description: This is a modern 'Nodes View' dashboard for your Kubernetes cluster(s). Made for kube-prometheus-stack and take advantage of the latest Grafana features. GitHub repository: https://github.com/dotdc/grafana-dashboards-kubernetes

spec.description: Number of times a CPU core has been throttled on an instance.
spec.title: Number of CPU Core Throttled

spec.description:
* nvme_nvme0 (Storage)
These are the sensors for your NVMe SSD.
 ** temp1 (Composite): The main temperature reported by the drive's firmware. This is the most important one for drive health.
 ** temp2 (Sensor 2): Usually represents a specific part of the drive, like the Flash Controller or the NAND memory chips. Controllers typically run hotter than the memory itself.

* platform_coretemp_0 (CPU)
This is the Intel Digital Thermal Sensor (DTS) on your processor.
 ** temp1 (Package): The temperature of the entire CPU package (the highest temperature recorded among all cores). This is the one you usually see in "single-value" CPU temp monitors.
 ** temp2 through temp5 (Cores): These represent the individual temperatures of each CPU Core (Core 0, Core 1, etc.). It’s normal for these to vary slightly from each other depending on which cores are currently
   doing the most work.

* thermal_thermal_zoneX (System/Motherboard)
These are generic "thermal zones" defined by the system's ACPI (BIOS/Firmware).
 ** thermal_zone0: On many systems, this is a redundant reading of the CPU Package, but reported via the motherboard's ACPI interface rather than directly from the CPU chip.
 ** thermal_zone3: This can represent various things depending on your hardware, but common ones are:
     *** PCH (Platform Controller Hub - the "chipset").
     *** Motherboard Ambient sensor.
     *** VRM (Voltage Regulator Modules) that power the CPU.
 ** Note: In your graph, thermal_thermal_zone0 - temp2 is that flat orange line at 30°C. This is likely a "static" or "limit" sensor that isn't actively changing.

Summary of what to watch:
 * CPU Health: Watch platform_coretemp_0 - temp1. If it's consistently above 85-90°C under load, you might have a cooling issue.
 * SSD Health: Watch nvme_nvme0 - temp1. Most NVMe drives start to throttle performance around 70°C.
 * Motherboard/Case Airflow: Watch the thermal_zone lines. If they climb steadily over time, it means heat is building up inside the case.
spec.title: Temperature
